questTitle: Sec 4 Chemistry: Periodic Table - Groups 1 & 17, Displacement & Redox
subject: Chemistry
description: Master the periodic table focusing on Group 1 (Alkali Metals) and Group 17 (Halogens)! This worksheet covers physical properties, chemical properties, displacement reactions, observations, and redox concepts. Read each question carefully.

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Group 1 - Physical Properties
questions.question: Group 1 elements are called alkali metals. Which of the following is NOT a Group 1 element?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Group 1 - Physical Properties
questions.question: Alkali metals are stored under oil. What property of alkali metals makes this necessary?
options: They react vigorously with air and water
questions.answer: They react vigorously with air and water

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Group 1 - Physical Properties
questions.question: How does the melting point change as you go DOWN Group 1 from lithium to caesium?
options: Melting point first increases then decreases

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Group 1 - Physical Properties
questions.question: Sodium, potassium, and lithium can float on water. What does this tell us about their density?
options: Their density is greater than water (>1 g/cm³)
Their density is less than water (<1 g/cm³)
Their density is exactly equal to water (1 g/cm³)
Density cannot be determined from this observation
questions.answer: Their density is less than water (<1 g/cm³)

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Group 1 - Physical Properties - Test Question
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the physical properties of Group 1 metals compared to typical transition metals?
options: Group 1 metals are harder and have higher melting points
Group 1 metals are softer and have lower melting points
Group 1 metals are denser and have higher boiling points
Group 1 metals are harder and have lower density
questions.answer: Group 1 metals are softer and have lower melting points

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Group 1 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: All Group 1 elements have one electron in their outermost shell. What type of ion do they form when they react?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Group 1 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: When sodium reacts with water, what products are formed?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Group 1 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: When potassium reacts with water, a lilac flame is observed. What causes this flame?
options: The hydrogen gas produced catches fire
questions.answer: The hydrogen gas produced catches fire

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Group 1 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: How does reactivity change as you go DOWN Group 1?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Group 1 - Chemical Properties - Test Question
questions.question: Potassium reacts more vigorously with water than sodium. Which explanation is correct?
options: Potassium has more protons attracting the outer electron
Potassium has more electron shells, so the outer electron is further from the nucleus and easier to lose
Potassium has fewer neutrons than sodium
Potassium has a smaller atomic radius than sodium
questions.answer: Potassium has more electron shells, so the outer electron is further from the nucleus and easier to lose

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Group 1 - Reactions and Observations
questions.question: Lithium is added to water. Which observation would you expect?
options: Lithium sinks and reacts slowly with no flame
Lithium floats, fizzes steadily, and moves around the surface
Lithium explodes immediately on contact with water
Lithium floats but does not react
questions.answer: Lithium floats, fizzes steadily, and moves around the surface

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Group 1 - Reactions and Observations
questions.question: Sodium is added to water. Which observation would you expect?
options: Sodium floats, melts into a ball, fizzes vigorously, and may catch fire
Sodium floats but does not react
Sodium explodes immediately with a loud bang
questions.answer: Sodium floats, melts into a ball, fizzes vigorously, and may catch fire

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Group 1 - Reactions and Observations
questions.question: After reacting sodium with water, universal indicator is added to the solution. What colour change would you observe?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Group 1 - Reactions and Observations
questions.question: The gas produced when alkali metals react with water can be tested. What is the test and expected result?
options: Lighted splint causes a 'pop' sound - hydrogen
Damp litmus paper turns white - chlorine
questions.answer: Lighted splint causes a 'pop' sound - hydrogen

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Group 1 - Reactions and Observations - Test Question
questions.question: Three alkali metals X, Y, and Z are added to water separately. X fizzes gently and floats. Y fizzes vigorously, melts into a ball, and catches fire with a lilac flame. Z fizzes steadily and moves around the surface. Arrange X, Y, and Z in order of increasing reactivity.

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Group 17 - Physical Properties
questions.question: Group 17 elements are called halogens. Which of the following is a halogen?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Group 17 - Physical Properties
questions.question: What is the physical state of chlorine at room temperature?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Group 17 - Physical Properties
questions.question: What is the physical state of bromine at room temperature?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Group 17 - Physical Properties
questions.question: How does the boiling point change as you go DOWN Group 17 from fluorine to iodine?
options: Boiling point first decreases then increases

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Group 17 - Physical Properties - Test Question
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the colour and state of the halogens at room temperature?
options: Chlorine: yellow-green gas; Bromine: red-brown liquid; Iodine: purple-black solid
Chlorine: colourless gas; Bromine: yellow liquid; Iodine: grey solid
Chlorine: green solid; Bromine: orange gas; Iodine: purple liquid
Chlorine: yellow-green liquid; Bromine: red-brown gas; Iodine: black gas
questions.answer: Chlorine: yellow-green gas; Bromine: red-brown liquid; Iodine: purple-black solid

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Group 17 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: All Group 17 elements have seven electrons in their outermost shell. What type of ion do they form when they react with metals?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Group 17 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: How does reactivity change as you go DOWN Group 17?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Group 17 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: Chlorine is more reactive than bromine. Which explanation is correct?
options: Chlorine has more electron shells, so it gains electrons more easily
Chlorine has fewer electron shells, so the incoming electron is closer to the nucleus and more strongly attracted
Chlorine has more protons than bromine
Chlorine has a larger atomic radius than bromine
questions.answer: Chlorine has fewer electron shells, so the incoming electron is closer to the nucleus and more strongly attracted

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Group 17 - Chemical Properties
questions.question: When chlorine reacts with sodium, what product is formed?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Group 17 - Chemical Properties - Test Question
questions.question: Fluorine is the most reactive halogen. Based on the trend in Group 17, which statement correctly explains this?
options: Fluorine has the most electron shells, making it easier to gain an electron
Fluorine has the smallest atomic radius, so the incoming electron experiences the strongest attraction to the nucleus
Fluorine has the most protons, giving it the strongest positive charge
Fluorine has the weakest intermolecular forces
questions.answer: Fluorine has the smallest atomic radius, so the incoming electron experiences the strongest attraction to the nucleus

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Halogen Displacement Reactions - Basics
questions.question: In a displacement reaction involving halogens, which halogen will displace another?
options: A less reactive halogen displaces a more reactive halogen
A more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive halogen
Any halogen can displace any other halogen
questions.answer: A more reactive halogen displaces a less reactive halogen

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Halogen Displacement Reactions - Basics
questions.question: Chlorine water is added to potassium bromide solution. What would you observe?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Halogen Displacement Reactions - Basics
questions.question: In the reaction between chlorine and potassium bromide, what is the word equation?
options: Chlorine + Potassium bromide → Potassium chloride + Bromine
Chlorine + Potassium bromide → Potassium + Chlorine bromide
Bromine + Potassium chloride → Potassium bromide + Chlorine
questions.answer: Chlorine + Potassium bromide → Potassium chloride + Bromine

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Halogen Displacement Reactions - Basics
questions.question: Bromine water is added to potassium chloride solution. What would you observe?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Halogen Displacement Reactions - Basics - Test Question
questions.question: Chlorine water is added to potassium iodide solution. Which observation and explanation is correct?
options: No change; chlorine is less reactive than iodine
Solution turns brown; chlorine displaces iodine as chlorine is more reactive
Solution turns green; iodine displaces chlorine
White precipitate forms; potassium chloride is insoluble
questions.answer: Solution turns brown; chlorine displaces iodine as chlorine is more reactive

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Displacement Reactions and Redox
questions.question: In the reaction Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂, what happens to chlorine?
options: Chlorine is neither oxidised nor reduced

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Displacement Reactions and Redox
questions.question: In the reaction Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂, what happens to bromide ions (Br⁻)?
options: Bromide ions are neither oxidised nor reduced
Bromide ions act as a catalyst

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Displacement Reactions and Redox
questions.question: In a redox reaction, the substance that causes another substance to be oxidised is called:

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Displacement Reactions and Redox
questions.question: In the reaction Cl₂ + 2Br⁻ → 2Cl⁻ + Br₂, what is the oxidising agent?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Displacement Reactions and Redox - Test Question
questions.question: In the ionic equation Cl₂ + 2I⁻ → 2Cl⁻ + I₂, identify the correct statements: (i) Cl₂ is reduced (ii) I⁻ is the reducing agent (iii) Cl₂ is the oxidising agent (iv) I₂ is oxidised

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Comparing Group 1 and Group 17 Trends
questions.question: As you go DOWN Group 1, reactivity increases. As you go DOWN Group 17, reactivity decreases. What is the reason for this opposite trend?
options: Group 1 metals lose electrons while Group 17 non-metals gain electrons
Group 1 metals gain electrons while Group 17 non-metals lose electrons
Both groups lose electrons but in different ways
The number of protons changes differently in each group
questions.answer: Group 1 metals lose electrons while Group 17 non-metals gain electrons

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Comparing Group 1 and Group 17 Trends
questions.question: When sodium reacts with chlorine, sodium chloride is formed. In this reaction, what happens to sodium?
options: Sodium is neither oxidised nor reduced

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Comparing Group 1 and Group 17 Trends
questions.question: In the reaction 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, what is the reducing agent?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Comparing Group 1 and Group 17 Trends
questions.question: Which pair of elements would react most vigorously with each other?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Comparing Group 1 and Group 17 Trends - Test Question
questions.question: Element X is in Group 1 and reacts vigorously with water, producing a lilac flame. Element Y is in Group 17 and is a red-brown liquid at room temperature. When X and Y react together: (i) Identify elements X and Y (ii) What type of bonding is in the product?
options: X is potassium, Y is bromine; ionic bonding
X is sodium, Y is iodine; covalent bonding
X is lithium, Y is chlorine; ionic bonding
X is potassium, Y is chlorine; covalent bonding
questions.answer: X is potassium, Y is bromine; ionic bonding
